name: Karen Palmer
title: Senior Fellow, Electricity & Environment
relevance: Leading energy economist studying electricity regulation and utility rate design. RFF is the preeminent DC think tank for energy policy research. Cross-state rate case data would directly support her empirical work on regulatory outcomes.
notes: Has published extensively on clean energy standards, electricity markets, and public utility regulation. Co-directs RFF's electricity program. Would benefit from structured rate case decision data for comparative regulatory analysis.

name: Jenya Kahn-Lang
title: Fellow, Electric Power Sector
relevance: Economist specializing in utility regulation, retail electricity markets, and rate design. PhD from UC Berkeley in Agricultural and Resource Economics. Her research on retail rate design and distributed energy resources directly intersects with cross-state rate case analysis.
notes: Newer fellow at RFF, likely building research agenda and looking for novel datasets. Background in both consulting and academic research on utility regulation makes her an ideal collaborator.

name: Severin Borenstein
title: E.T. Grether Professor of Business Administration and Public Policy; Faculty Director, Energy Institute at Haas
organization: UC Berkeley Haas School of Business
relevance: One of the most cited energy economists in the world. Directs the Energy Institute at Haas. His research on electricity markets, utility pricing, and the distributional effects of energy policy would benefit enormously from structured, cross-state rate case data.
source: UC Berkeley Haas faculty page — faculty.haas.berkeley.edu/borenste/
notes: Extremely high-profile. A collaboration or data citation from Borenstein would be a major credibility signal. Has co-authored with Lucas Davis on utility rate topics. Approach with well-polished sample data and clear methodology.

name: Christopher Knittel
title: George P. Shultz Professor of Applied Economics; Director, Center for Energy and Environmental Policy Research (CEEPR)
relevance: Directs MIT's CEEPR, the leading academic center for energy policy research. His work on environmental economics and industrial organization intersects directly with utility regulation and rate-setting. Cross-state PUC data would be valuable for CEEPR working papers.
notes: CEEPR produces influential working papers on electricity markets. A dataset partnership or citation in a CEEPR publication would have significant academic and industry reach.

name: Ethan Howland
title: Senior Reporter, FERC and Electricity Markets
relevance: Covers FERC, electricity markets, and utility regulation for Utility Dive — the most-read trade publication in the utility industry. Has covered rate cases, ROE disputes, and transmission cost recovery extensively for two decades.
notes: Portland, ME based. His FERC and rate case coverage makes him the single best journalist to pitch this dataset to. A Utility Dive feature on the tracker would reach every utility executive and regulator in the country.

name: Robert Walton
title: Reporter, Energy Policy and Utility Demand
relevance: Covers state utility regulation, rate cases, and energy policy for Utility Dive. 15 years covering policy and regulation in Washington, D.C. His state-level regulatory focus aligns perfectly with a cross-state PUC rate case database.
notes: State-level regulatory coverage is his specialty. Pitch with specific cross-state comparisons (e.g., how ROE decisions differ across PA vs. CA vs. OR) that could serve as story angles.

name: Jeff St. John
title: Chief Reporter and Policy Specialist
relevance: Chief reporter at Canary Media covering the intersection of technology, economics, and regulation in the clean energy transition. Former managing editor at Greentech Media. UC Berkeley journalism grad. Covers how regulatory decisions affect grid modernization.
notes: Canary Media merged with Energy News Network in 2025, expanding state/local coverage. Jeff's policy angle means he'd be interested in data showing how rate case decisions accelerate or hinder clean energy investment across states.

name: Peter Behr
title: Senior Energy Reporter
relevance: Senior energy reporter at E&E News Energywire covering power grid reliability, climate policy, and utility regulation since 2009. E&E News is the energy policy publication of record in Washington. Part of Politico's environment and energy reporting operation.
notes: E&E News reaches policymakers, regulators, and industry executives. Pitch with a DC policy angle — how rate case outcomes vary across states and what that means for federal energy goals.

name: ScottMadden (Energy Practice)
title: Energy Regulation & Rates Practice
relevance: Leading utility management consulting firm specializing in rate case management and regulatory strategy. Has been engaged in 350+ proceedings before utility commissions in 43 states. Acquired Sussex Economic Advisors to expand ratemaking expertise. Would use cross-state rate case data for benchmarking and client advisory.
notes: Their rate case practice explicitly needs cross-state regulatory comparison data. Current data likely comes from S&P RRA at significant cost. A free/affordable structured alternative would be compelling. Pitch to energy practice leadership.

name: Concentric Energy Advisors
title: Regulatory and Financial Advisory
relevance: Utility regulatory consulting firm whose experts provide testimony in nearly all U.S. states and before FERC. Focuses exclusively on North American energy and water industries. Cross-state rate case data would support their regulatory benchmarking, expert testimony preparation, and client advisory work.
notes: Smaller and more specialized than ScottMadden. Their expert witnesses need rate case precedent data across jurisdictions. Offer a sample dataset showing rate case outcomes for their key client states.

name: Halcyon
title: AI-Powered Regulatory Intelligence Platform
relevance: Startup ($10.8M seed) using AI to structure regulatory filings from FERC and DOE. They are building exactly what this project demonstrates — structured regulatory data from unstructured filings. Nathan's PUC rate case tracker proves he can build the core data extraction pipelines they need.
notes: Early-stage startup that would value a candidate who has already built a working regulatory data extraction system. Pitch as both a data partnership opportunity and a hiring conversation. Show the working pipeline and dashboard.

name: Arcadia
title: Utility Data Platform
relevance: Utility data platform that translates tariff sheets, utility bills, and market pricing into actionable intelligence. Their core business is structuring messy utility data. Nathan's rate case tracker demonstrates precisely this skill — extracting structured data from inconsistent PUC filings across multiple states.
notes: Larger company with established platform. Best approached with the rate case tracker as a portfolio project demonstrating data engineering and regulatory domain expertise. Look for data engineering or product roles.

name: Christopher Casey
title: Utility Regulatory Director, New York
relevance: Leads NRDC's utility regulatory work in New York, including interventions at the NY PSC and FERC. Former energy attorney and assistant attorney general at Washington State UTC. NRDC intervenes in rate cases nationally and needs cross-state benchmarking data to support their advocacy positions.
notes: NRDC is a major intervenor in rate cases across the country. They would use cross-state data to argue that utilities in one state are seeking excessive returns compared to what other commissions have approved. A data partnership with NRDC would provide significant credibility and distribution.

name: Sierra Club Beyond Coal Campaign
title: Beyond Coal / Beyond Dirty Fuels Campaign
relevance: In 2025, the Sierra Club engaged in 120+ PUC cases across 30+ state commissions, fighting utility rate increases tied to fossil fuel investments. Their legal and advocacy teams need exactly this data — structured cross-state rate case outcomes to compare utility requests vs. approved amounts.
notes: Sierra Club's state-based PUC engagement teams are the largest nongovernmental interveners in utility rate cases. They blocked rate increases in Utah (Rocky Mountain Power) and West Virginia (AEP) in 2025. Offer dataset access in exchange for distribution to their national advocacy network.

name: AARP Utility Advocacy Program
title: State Utility Rate Advocacy
relevance: AARP intervenes in utility rate cases in 17+ states to protect consumer interests. Scored 30+ utility wins in 2025 alone. Their state-level advocacy teams need cross-state benchmarking data to argue against excessive rate increases. AARP has called for full review of rate case expenses and more consumer representation.
notes: AARP's New York advocacy (led by Beth Finkel) has been especially vocal on utility rate reform. The organization wants to make rate case data more accessible to consumer groups. Pitch as a tool that empowers their state advocacy teams with cross-state comparison data.

name: National Consumer Law Center (NCLC)
title: Energy Utility Program
relevance: NCLC works on energy law and utility affordability for low-income consumers. In 2025, energy affordability became a major concern due to rising gas prices, data center demand, and infrastructure rebuild costs. NCLC needs structured rate case data to support legal advocacy and policy recommendations.
notes: Boston-based. Their energy program focuses on keeping low-income households connected to utility service. Cross-state rate case data would help them identify where rate increases disproportionately burden vulnerable consumers. Pitch as a research partnership.

name: Kari Lydersen
title: Contributing Reporter, Midwest Energy
relevance: Covers Illinois, Indiana, and Wisconsin energy policy for Canary Media. Indiana is one of the five states in the PUC Rate Case Tracker. Regional energy reporters need structured data for state-level stories about utility regulation and rate impacts.
notes: Chicago-based. Her Midwest coverage means she regularly writes about Indiana PUC decisions. Pitch with Indiana-specific data (e.g., Duke Energy Indiana rate case outcomes) and cross-state comparisons.
